title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: [37]Modern minimalism, in its most widely promoted and commercially packaged form, is little more than a carefully curated aesthetic for the privileged—a sophisticated way for those who already possess everything they could ever need to perform a stylized and photogenic version of deliberate poverty.[/37] The pervasive 'less is more' mantra typically involves replacing a quantity of affordable, perfectly functional everyday items with a carefully chosen collection of exorbitantly expensive, supposedly 'sustainably made' designer pieces. This is emphatically not a genuine rejection of consumerism; it is consumerism in its most refined, selective, and socially exclusive form—its ultimate expression rather than its antithesis. The moral framing of material ownership as a character flaw is the real philosophical problem here. [39]True and meaningful simplicity should be about reducing our mental clutter and excessive social obligations and expectations, not about the approved color of our walls or the prescribed number of spoons in our kitchen drawer.[/39] Authentic inner freedom matters far more than outward aesthetic conformity.
texts.B: The genuine psychological benefits of a thoughtfully decluttered and purposefully organized living environment are consistently and clearly supported by a growing body of robust empirical evidence from cognitive science and environmental psychology. Our human brains are simply not designed to efficiently process the constant and relentless visual and cognitive noise of the contemporary digital and physical landscape. By intentionally and deliberately limiting our possessions to what we genuinely need and value, we reclaim the mental capacity for sustained deep focus, authentic human connection, and genuine creative thought. [40]While a vocal minority may certainly use minimalism as a sophisticated performance of cultural status, for the vast majority of genuine adherents it represents a necessary and deeply healthy survival strategy in an age of overwhelming stimulation and constant distraction.[/40] [38]It is fundamentally a practical tool for protecting and improving mental health, not merely a passing aesthetic trend promoted by interior designers and luxury lifestyle publications.[/38]
texts.C: I share the reasonable skepticism directed at the overly aestheticized and heavily commercialized version of the so-called 'minimalist ideal' that dominates popular media. [39]A perfectly white room containing a single carefully chosen designer chair is not a cultural or political revolution; it is a professionally staged photograph suitable for a luxury lifestyle publication.[/39] However, [37]I firmly disagree with the characterization that minimalism is inherently or necessarily elitist in its deepest philosophical intentions. At its authentic core, it represents a genuinely powerful and timely philosophical critique of the unsustainable, debt-fueled economic growth model that underpins our entire consumer society.[/37] By deliberately and consciously choosing to want less, we collectively reclaim our most finite and precious resource—time itself—from the exhausting and ultimately empty 'work-to-spend' treadmill. The meaningful focus should always be on the 'intentionality' and genuine awareness behind our choices rather than a rigid numerical count of possessions. It is ultimately about reclaiming authentic personal agency in a system designed to deny it.
texts.D: Minimalism as a widespread cultural movement represents a predictable and historically familiar reaction to the material and spiritual excesses of the late twentieth century, but it increasingly risks becoming its own peculiar form of rigid and anxiety-inducing perfectionism. [40]The relentless social pressure to achieve and maintain a 'perfectly curated' minimalist life can paradoxically prove just as psychologically stressful and exhausting as the very domestic disorder and material accumulation it ostensibly seeks to replace.[/40] [38]I agree that thoughtfully reducing our physical consumption footprint is both ecologically responsible and potentially personally liberating, but we must firmly resist the temptation to moralize other people's possessions, lifestyle choices, and domestic arrangements.[/38] Owning things that genuinely bring joy and practical utility does not make one a morally inferior person. Furthermore, the popular practice of systematically 'getting rid of everything' is frequently quite wasteful—discarding perfectly functional items only to eventually replace them with approved 'minimalist' alternatives purchased from the same consumer economy.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on whether minimalism is essentially a form of elitist consumerism?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls it 'elitist' and a 'refined form of consumerism.' Expert C disagrees, arguing it is a 'powerful critique' of the economic model and a way to reclaim time.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's opinion about the mental health benefits of reducing clutter?
questions.explanation: Expert B highlights the 'psychological benefits' and 'mental health.' Expert D also notes that reducing the physical footprint is 'ecologically responsible' and agrees that excessive clutter creates 'mess' and stress.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to A regarding the superficiality of the minimalist 'look'?
questions.explanation: Expert A criticizes the focus on the 'color of our walls.' Expert C shares this skepticism, calling the 'white room with a designer chair' just a photograph and not a true revolution.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the emotional impact of striving for a minimalist lifestyle?
questions.explanation: Expert D warns that minimalism can become 'rigid perfectionism' and a source of stress. Expert B argues it is a 'necessary survival strategy' that helps regain focus and connection.
